rationale: This reaction is a classic example of the Houben-Hoesch synthesis, demonstrating the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (acetophenone derivative). This example is simple, uses common starting materials, and clearly illustrates the key C-C bond formation and functional group transformation characteristic of the reaction.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a highly activated polyalkoxybenzene, which is characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction and distinguishes it from the more general Friedel-Crafts acylation that typically uses acyl halides or anhydrides. The use of a nitrile as the acylating agent is the defining feature. The simple, acyclic nitrile and the specific aromatic substrate avoid overlap with other named reactions involving complex ring systems or different functional groups.
